step.title: Draft Design Proposal
step.goal: Compose the brain-surgery proposal: the artifact chain this work requires, the decision points future user touchpoints will surface, and the risk areas where friction is most likely. Tag recipe drives depth.

substep.title: Load Tag Recipe (if available)
substep.action:
Check whether a tag-recipe file exists at .vera/shared/references/tag-recipes/{{session.primary_tag}}.md (these are Phase 4 references; may not exist yet during ticketsmith development). If present, load it; it provides depth guidance for the primary tag.
Tag-recipe contents (when authored) specify: - Depth expectation for D-phase per tag - Mandatory artifact-chain elements per tag - Typical risk profile per tag
If absent, use built-in defaults based on the spec §8 tag descriptions: feature → full depth, bug → medium, exploration → learning-focused, hotfix → minimal, infrastructure → medium, chore → minimal.
Store {{session.tag_recipe}} (or "default" if no file found).

substep.title: Identify the Artifact Chain
substep.action:
Based on the Q↔R findings and the tag recipe, identify the downstream artifacts that will need creating or modifying to deliver this work. Categories:
- **DRs** (`kind: decision`): hard-to-reverse decisions that will need recording. Some may already be linked from prior turns; new ones may surface during structure or implementation. - **Specs** (`kind: spec`, if the spec layer exists in the target harness): formal contracts for the behavior. - **Sub-tickets**: if Q↔R revealed multi-slice scope, the implementation-QRDSPIV will decompose. Surface that here. - **Live document updates**: brief, architecture, policy, manifest may need touching. - **Implementation**: the code that delivers the behavior. - **Tests / verification**: if not implicit in implementation.
Per the worked example in QRDSPIV: a feature ticket's chain typically reads "DR → Brief update → ADR → Architecture update → Spec → Implementation."
Order the chain by dependency. Store as {{session.artifact_chain}}.
IMPORTANT: the artifact chain is SCOPING, not prescription. The agent is identifying what artifacts will be needed, not specifying their content. Per QRDSPIV §S: "Scope, don't prescribe."

substep.title: Identify Decision Points
substep.action:
Walk through the artifact chain. For each artifact, identify whether the user will need to make a decision during its creation that goes beyond mechanical work:
- ADR creation typically requires a user decision on the chosen alternative - Spec creation may surface edge-case decisions - Implementation may surface technology-version or library decisions
List decision points as: "Step N (artifact X): user decides Y."
Store as {{session.decision_points}}. This is the calendar of future user touchpoints that downstream implementation-QRDSPIV will need to honor.

substep.title: Identify Risk Areas
substep.action:
Synthesize from the research findings: where is the friction most likely to surface? Risk areas typically include:
- Cross-cutting code touch (e.g., "auth middleware touches 47 test files") - Migration steps (data migration, schema change, API version bump) - Permissions or security implications - Performance-critical paths - Third-party dependency surfaces (vendor APIs, OS-level) - Breaking changes to public contracts
Be specific. Cite the research findings that surface each risk (file paths, prior-art notes from the research file).
Store as {{session.risk_areas}}: list of {area, evidence, impact-if-mismanaged}.

substep.title: Draft the Design Summary Block
Display Draft for Internal Review

section.title: Scoping, not prescribing
section.description:
Per QRDSPIV §S: "At the ticket level, S stays high-level. The ticket creator does not need to know the internal structure of each artifact type. 'We need a spec covering teams, membership, and permissions' is correct scoping. 'The spec needs 6 Kipling categories with these specific criteria' is too deep — that's the spec creator's job during the spec's own S phase."
Same principle applies to D at the ticket-writing layer. D-phase identifies WHAT artifacts and decisions are needed — not what the content of each artifact will be.

rules.rule: The Design Summary does NOT name implementation libraries unless they are constraints (e.g., "must use Sequelize because that's what the codebase uses"). Library selection during implementation is implementation-QRDSPIV's job
The Design Summary does NOT specify file paths for new code
The Design Summary does NOT prescribe vertical slices — that's the implementation-QRDSPIV's P-phase
The Design Summary DOES cite specific files and modules from the research findings when surfacing risk (those are evidence, not prescription)

section.title: Risk-area evidence discipline
section.description: Every risk area cites specific evidence from the research file. "Auth middleware refactor is risky" without evidence is hand-waving. "Auth middleware (src/middleware/auth.ts:42) is referenced by 38 route handlers per the codebase-locator finding in research file Turn 4; refactoring will require updating each handler's req.user.id usage" is grounded.

section.title: What if the artifact chain reveals decomposition
section.description:
Per spec §9, ticket-writing produces ONE ticket per session. Decomposition into sub-tickets is implementation-QRDSPIV's job (its P-phase). D-phase surfaces the LIKELY decomposition as a risk-area or in the artifact-chain narrative, but does NOT produce sub-tickets here.
Example: "The implementation step is likely to decompose into 3-4 vertical slices (model layer, middleware, route handlers, test migration) — this is downstream P-phase's job, not this ticket's scope."
